name: Internal environment disorders
body:
**Internal environment disorders** group together fluid, electrolyte, and acid-base disturbances frequently encountered in the emergency department: hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypokalemia, hypoglycemia, hypocalcemia, and acid-base balance disorders. They are usually the **consequence** of another underlying pathology (vomiting, diarrhea, diuretics, AKI, sepsis, excessive insulin), and inappropriate correction can be iatrogenic.

**Hypovolemic dehydration** presents with thirst, dry mucous membranes, orthostatic hypotension, oliguria, and hemoconcentration; treatment consists of fluid resuscitation with crystalloids according to severity and cause. **Symptomatic hyponatremia** (seizures, coma) requires controlled correction with hypertonic saline according to protocol; rapid correction must be avoided to prevent myelinolysis. **Severe hyperkalemia** demands membrane stabilization (IV calcium), intracellular shift (insulin-dextrose, beta-2 agonists), and elimination depending on available resources.

**Nursing:** monitor vital signs, urine output, and capillary blood glucose if applicable; administer electrolytes and intravenous fluids using an infusion pump when appropriate; and repeat laboratory tests according to medical orders. Document the volume administered and the clinical response.

bibliography.citation: European Renal Association. Clinical practice guideline on management of hyponatraemia.
Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO). Electrolyte disorders.
Ministerio de Salud de la Nación Argentina. Estrategias y guías de atención.
American Society of Nephrology. Core curriculum in electrolyte disorders.
